turns.text: Hello Antionette, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Oh, thank you. I appreciate you saying that. Honestly, it’s a bit strange to sit here and talk about it—usually, I’m the one on the other side, taking notes, reading the latest research on fetal development. 

I’m thirty-one, and this is my third, but it feels like my first in so many ways. I think that’s because this time, they’ve labeled me "medium risk." I know, logically, that my first two births were textbook, but that label feels like a heavy weight I’m carrying around Worcester every single day. I find myself obsessing over the details—tracking my protein intake, reading about lung maturation, wondering if my glucose is where it needs to be. 

Sometimes I feel like I’m sitting for an exam when I walk into my OB’s office. I bring my lists of questions, and I want to be the "perfect" patient, but inside? I’m terrified. I’m scared that by focusing so hard on the data and the milestones, I’m somehow missing the actual experience of being pregnant. I want to trust my body, but I keep looking for signs of distress because I’m so afraid of failing. It’s exhausting, really. Does that make sense? It feels like I’m balancing on a tightrope between being a well-informed mother and just... a person who needs to be held.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

That’s a complicated question for me. I have this midwife, and she’s lovely—she’s incredibly kind and always tells me, "Antionette, you’re doing great," but I struggle to really let those words sink in. When she says that, a part of me wants to cry, but another part of me just feels like she’s reciting a script. It’s like there’s this wall; I’m so deep into the clinical details of my own charts that I feel like a spectator in my own pregnancy. I’m analyzing the data, and she’s looking at the person, and we’re just... missing each other.

Then there’s my OB. With her, it’s all business. It’s professional, it’s precise, and on one hand, that’s exactly what I think I want because it matches my own obsession with the "science" of it all. But when I leave that office, I feel more like a case file than a woman about to bring a new life into the world. 

I think the relationship I’m actually missing is the one where I can let my guard down. I’m so busy trying to be the informed, prepared, "medium risk" patient that I don't give them space to just see me as someone who’s scared. I’m protecting myself with knowledge, I guess, but it’s keeping me from really connecting with the people who are supposed to be taking care of me.
Who else was involved in your care that you'd like to mention — community midwives, consultants, support workers, interpreters?

To be honest, it’s mostly just been the clinical team. Outside of the OB and the midwife, my world is pretty small—just me, my husband, and my two older kids running around the house. My husband is great, but he’s in this "let’s just get to the finish line" mode, and he keeps telling me I’m over-preparing. He thinks I’m worrying about things that haven’t happened, and maybe he’s right, but he doesn't see the journals I'm reading or the way I panic if I haven't felt the baby move in a specific rhythm for an hour.

I haven't really looked into community midwives or doulas, even though I probably should. I think a part of me is afraid that if I bring in someone else, they’ll just see right through this "composed, educated patient" mask I’ve built. If I talk to a consultant or someone who isn't strictly focused on the clinical protocol, I might actually have to admit how much this "medium risk" label is eating me alive. 

It’s silly, isn't it? I have access to all this information, and yet I’m sitting here feeling like I’m doing this whole thing in isolation. I don't feel like I have a "village"—I feel like I have a medical chart and a to-do list.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

My husband is a wonderful man, and he’s been through two births with me, so he’s very much in the mindset of "everything went fine before, it’ll go fine again." He’s my rock, really, but it’s a double-edged sword. When I’m spiraling over a blood sugar reading or a nuance in my recent ultrasound, he tries to pull me back to reality by saying, "Antionette, look at us. We’re tired, we’re busy, but we’re healthy."

He’s physically present for the big scans, but the day-to-day? He doesn’t see the internal monologue. I don't think he realizes that when I’m staring at my phone, I’m not just scrolling social media—I’m researching neonatal heart rate variability or whatever new "medium risk" anxiety has hit me that morning. 

In some ways, his calmness makes me feel more isolated. Because he isn't worried, I feel like my anxiety is an indulgence, or worse, a sign that I’m not handling this third pregnancy with the "experience" I should have. I find myself hiding my lists, or closing my laptop when he walks into the kitchen, because I don't want to hear "you're overthinking it" again. I want him to worry, just a little, so I don't feel like I’m the only one guarding this baby’s safety.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

Honestly? I don't really turn to my friends or family for this. My friends are either in a completely different stage of life, or their experiences were so "easy" that I feel like a frantic mess when I talk to them. I don't want to be the "anxious pregnant friend." There’s a pressure to be the one who has it all together, especially since this is my third. If I told my mom I was obsessing over myelination patterns, she’d just tell me to eat a cracker and lie down. She’s right, probably, but it doesn't help the ache in my chest.

So, I’ve mostly turned to online forums—anonymous ones. It’s strange, right? I spend my nights reading these long, technical threads from other women who are in the same "medium risk" boat. Seeing someone else analyze their data makes me feel less like I’m losing my mind. But it’s a dangerous cycle. I find comfort in the data, but the data is what fuels the fear. 

It never really integrates with my formal care. When I go to my OB, I’d never bring up what I read on some forum at 2:00 AM. I’m scared they’d think I’m one of "those" patients—the one who googles everything. So, I keep my professional life and my secret, anxious life in two separate silos. It’s like living a double life, just waiting for the day I finally give birth so I can stop feeling like I’m studying for a final exam.
Who else was involved that perhaps you didn't expect to lean on — an online group, a work colleague, a community figure?

You know, it’s funny you ask that. I didn’t expect to lean on my boss, of all people. She’s a no-nonsense type, very clinical in her own work, but when I had to take a half-day for an extra monitoring appointment recently, she just looked at me and said, "Antionette, don't apologize. Just take the time you need." 

It wasn't even about the time off; it was the fact that she didn’t ask for a medical justification. In my professional life, I’m used to explaining *why* something is happening, citing sources, and justifying my existence. To just have someone say "take care of yourself" without needing a lecture on the science of my pregnancy was surprisingly grounding. 

I also found myself talking to a woman I’ve seen at the local playground a few times. She’s pregnant, too, and she’s definitely not the "researcher" type. We were just sitting there watching our kids, and she mentioned she was tired of being poked and prodded. She didn't know the terminology, she wasn't tracking her protein, she was just... exhausted. I didn't tell her I was "medium risk" or that I knew exactly what her baby’s lungs were doing at that moment. I just nodded and said, "Yeah, I get that." For a few minutes, I wasn't the patient or the scholar. I was just another mom at the park. It was a relief, honestly.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

There was this moment a few weeks ago—a decision about an extra, non-routine fetal echo. Because of the "medium risk" label, they wanted to be "thorough." When the doctor brought it up, I felt this immediate, sharp divide in my brain. My logical side knew it was just a precautionary measure, but my anxious side translated it as: *They see something. They know something is wrong.*

I remember sitting in the exam chair, staring at the sterile white paper, feeling like my pulse was audible in the room. I asked a dozen questions about sensitivity and specificity, the odds of a false positive, and the exact clinical rationale. The doctor was patient, but she was treating it like a textbook discussion. 

My husband was there, and he just looked at me, squeezed my hand, and said, "If it gives you peace of mind, just do it." 

That felt… heavy. Because it wasn't about the baby's health at that point—the clinical risk was low. It was about my own mental stability. I felt this profound pressure to make the "right" choice for the baby, while simultaneously knowing that the choice was really just to manage my own panic. I agreed to the scan, but I walked out feeling a strange mix of relief and resentment. Relief that I’d have the data, but resentment that I’d been pushed to a place where I felt I had to choose between my sanity and the clinical machinery. I made the decision, but I felt like the decision was just a bandage on a wound that no one else in that room could see.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

It’s funny you mention pressure. I think the biggest pressure comes from the "super-mom" narrative in my own head, but my workplace definitely mirrors it. Being full-time while navigating this third trimester is… well, it’s a lot. I’ve kept my head down, keeping up with deadlines and pretending that the fatigue isn't hitting me by 2:00 PM. I feel like if I show any weakness, or if I admit that my "medium risk" status is making me distracted, I’ll lose my seat at the table. It’s that old fear of being viewed as less capable because I’m growing a human.

My community? It’s a bit of a void. We live in a neighborhood where everyone is busy, focused on their own families. I don’t belong to a faith group, and sometimes I think that’s where the isolation hits the hardest. I don't have that built-in space to just "be" or to receive support without it being a transaction. 

The pressure isn't necessarily someone coming up to me and saying, "You need to be better." It’s the silence of being alone in my own ambitions. I want to be the professional, the loving mother of two, the well-read patient, and the one who keeps a clean, organized house. Trying to hold all those roles up—it’s like I’m constantly waiting for one of them to drop. I wish there was a space where I could be "less than" and still feel like I was doing it right.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

Every single day. It’s like living in two different dimensions. There’s the Antionette who is a professional, who values logic, data, and competence; and then there’s this version of me that’s being entirely reshaped by pregnancy—literally and figuratively. 

The tension is constant. When I’m at work, I’m trying to keep my brain sharp, but there’s this physical reality of the baby moving, or my lungs feeling compressed, that makes me feel like I’m "leaking" out of my professional container. And then at the OB’s office, the tables turn: I try to be the analytical, prepared peer to the doctor, but the "medium risk" label makes me feel like a child again, someone who needs to be told what’s safe and what’s not.

I hate that I feel like I’m losing the "me" who existed before this pregnancy. I used to be someone who solved problems, not someone who *was* the problem to be solved. My identity feels like it’s being subsumed by this medical case file. Sometimes I wonder if, once this baby is here, I’ll be able to find the person who didn’t obsess over protein goals or heart rate variability. It’s like I’m mourning my own independence while waiting for someone else’s to begin. I’m just so tired of the duality.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

It’s never just a physical exam; it’s an interrogation. When the ultrasound wand goes over my stomach, I’m not just lying there; I’m staring at the screen, looking for the same things the technician is looking for, trying to interpret the shadows before she speaks. It’s invasive, not just physically, but psychologically. 

When my OB is there, she’s focused on the measurements—the growth percentiles, the Doppler flows. I feel this urge to perform, to ask the "right" questions so I don't appear ignorant, even though I’m usually trembling inside. If my husband is in the room, I feel this sudden, sharp protective instinct. I don't want him to see me as the "patient." I want to remain the person who has it handled, so I often find myself making small talk to cover the fact that I’m holding my breath, waiting for the words "everything looks fine."

The worst moments are the silence. When they’re clicking away at the keyboard and the room goes quiet, I feel like a specimen on a slide. I’m being measured against a standard, and I’m just waiting to be told if I’ve passed or failed. I’m stripped of my autonomy in those minutes. I am just a uterus, a glucose reading, and a "medium risk" statistic, and I absolutely hate that I can’t just be a woman waiting to meet her child.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

There have been times when I’ve explicitly *not* wanted my husband there, which sounds terrible when I say it out loud, but it’s the truth. When the doctor starts talking about the “medium risk” markers or potential complications, I feel this intense, primal need to be a wall—to be the one who absorbs the information and processes it before anyone else gets scared. If he’s there, I have to manage his reaction, too. I have to be the one to say, "It’s okay, it’s standard procedure," even if I’m terrified myself.

Being alone, oddly enough, makes me feel more exposed but also more capable of being "the patient." When I’m by myself, I can drop the mask of the composed, knowledgeable mom. I can let my lip quiver. I can ask a question that sounds stupid, or admit that I didn't sleep at all because I was counting kicks. 

But then, there are the moments where the loneliness is just too sharp. Like the last time I had to do a glucose test—I sat in that waiting room feeling like I was failing at the most basic biological function. In those moments, I craved someone—anyone—to just hold my hand and tell me I wasn't just a clinical data point. I felt so exposed, sitting there in my scrubs, feeling like the entire system was just waiting for me to hit a "high risk" threshold. It’s a strange balance; I need them there to feel safe, but their presence forces me to keep up a performance I’m exhausted by.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

Honestly, I navigated it entirely on my own. That’s the "researcher" in me, I suppose. I’m the one who reads the hospital protocols, cross-references the latest clinical guidelines, and marks my calendar with military precision. I haven't asked anyone for help because I don't trust that anyone else would be as thorough as I need to be. If I’m going to be "medium risk," I’m going to be the most informed "medium risk" patient they have.

But that’s exactly what makes it so hard. I’ve become my own navigator, which is exhausting. When I’m sitting there, clicking through the insurance portal, trying to figure out which specialists are in-network, it feels like I’m managing a project, not a pregnancy. 

The people who make it harder are the ones who tell me to "relax and let the doctors handle it." I want to scream when they say that. It feels like they’re asking me to hand over the keys to a car while it’s still moving. I can’t "let them handle it" because I don't feel like they see *me*. They see the label. I’ve had to fight for every piece of information I have, and while it makes me feel prepared, it also makes me feel incredibly, painfully isolated. I’m navigating a system that treats me like a list of symptoms, and I’m the only one looking out for the person attached to them.
Who else was involved in translating the system for you — a friend who had been through it, an online group, a community health worker?

The only "translators" I’ve had are the ones I’ve found in those late-night, silent hours on the internet. It’s pathetic, maybe, but I’ve found these groups where women share their actual experiences with the same providers or similar clinical scenarios—the "real" version of what happens behind the clinic doors. They tell me what to expect during a growth scan, what the nurses might ask, and—more importantly—how to phrase my questions so I don't get dismissed as just another "anxious" woman. 

In a way, they’ve been more helpful than the official pamphlets or the hospital website. They’ve helped me decode the medical jargon that my doctor uses like a shorthand. But even then, there’s a distance. These women are strangers, and while they understand the *fears*, they don't know the *person*. 

I’ve never had a community health worker or someone to guide me through the labyrinth of the maternity system. I think if I had, maybe I wouldn’t feel like I’m constantly white-knuckling my way through this. Maybe I’d be able to focus on the baby’s movements instead of the clinical protocol. I’m doing all this translation work myself, trying to bridge the gap between "Antionette the person" and "Antionette the medical case." It’s exhausting, and some days, I feel like I’m running out of ink for all the notes I’m taking.
Tell me about the people who were with you — or who you wished had been with you — during your labour and birth. Who did each person support, and what difference did their presence make?

Looking back at my previous two births, I remember the room being filled with people I thought I *needed*—doctors, nurses, my husband. They were all there for the clinical event. But thinking about this third birth, I’m realizing that "support" is not what I got. I got management. I got monitoring. 

I’ve been fantasizing about having someone there who is solely for *me*—not for the baby’s vitals, not for the hospital’s protocols, just for me. My husband is wonderful, but he’s already "team baby." He’s focused on the outcome, the finish line. He’s not equipped to hold the space for my fear. I’ve caught myself wishing for a doula or a midwife who would just sit in the corner and remind me that I am more than the fetal heart rate trace. Someone who doesn't care about my charts but cares that I’m exhausted and scared.

I’m terrified that this time, I’ll be even more isolated. The "medium risk" label makes me feel like I’ll be surrounded by monitors and people waiting for something to go wrong. I want someone in that room who sees the human in the bed, not the patient file. I just want to be held while I’m doing the hardest work of my life, without having to explain why I’m feeling the way I am. Is that too much to ask?
Tell me about the midwives and doctors who cared for you during your birth. Were there particular individuals whose presence or approach made a lasting impression — positive or negative?

I remember my last birth so vividly, not because of the joy, but because of a specific nurse. She was so efficient, so clinical. Every time I felt a wave of intensity, she was right there with the blood pressure cuff or checking the monitor, her face a neutral mask of professional focus. I remember wanting to just *collapse* into her, to have her tell me I was doing a good job—not in a medical sense, but in a human one. Instead, she just muttered, "Good, your levels are steady," and walked out to update the chart. 

It left such a hollow feeling in me. It wasn’t that she was mean; she was technically excellent. But her presence made me feel like an object being maintained rather than a woman in the middle of a life-changing event. I felt like I had to perform "calm" for her, even while my body was doing things I couldn't control.

Then there was the midwife who came in right at the end. She didn't look at the screen first; she looked at my face. She said, "Antionette, look at me. Breathe." It was such a small thing, but it grounded me instantly. She saw *me* before she saw the monitors. That moment of recognition—where I wasn't just a "medium risk" file but a person—that’s what I’m clinging to for this time around. I just wish there were more of those moments and fewer of the ones where I feel like I’m being managed by a checklist.
Tell me about your partner's — or support person's — role during your birth. What did they bring to that experience, and what do you think they found most challenging?

My husband, Mark, he’s a doer. During the births, he’s always been the one tracking the time, holding the water, and giving me these firm, steady reminders to "just breathe through it." He’s a wonderful, steady force, but I think he finds the birth experience itself utterly terrifying. He tries to manage his own fear by keeping his eyes glued to the monitors, almost like he’s trying to be a second clinician in the room to make sure the hospital staff is doing their job. 

While that’s meant to be supportive, it makes me feel like I’m being watched from every angle—not just by the staff, but by the person who’s supposed to be my safe harbor. I think the challenge for him is that he wants to "fix" the pain, or at least control the outcome. When he sees me struggling, he gets this frantic, rigid look in his eyes that he thinks is "focused," but it translates to me as panic.

I sometimes wonder what it would be like if he could just sit with me in the mess of it, instead of trying to be my coach or my advocate. I don’t need him to manage the nurses; I need him to just be present with me while I’m doing the work. I think he finds it impossible to just exist in that vulnerability. He feels like if he stops monitoring the situation, something will go wrong. It’s hard to let go of that, for both of us.
How did the clinical team engage with your partner or support person — did they feel included as part of the care, or more like a bystander?

It’s interesting—the clinical team actually engages with Mark much more than they do with me, in some ways. They treat him like the “secondary pilot.” If the doctor needs to explain a protocol or a risk factor, they’ll look right at him, like he’s the one who needs to translate the data for the “patient.” It makes me feel completely sidelined, like I’m a passenger in my own delivery room.

On one hand, it’s a relief because he’s the one taking in all the technical details, but on the other, it makes me feel like a bystander in my own body. The team views him as the one to "manage" the situation, which only reinforces his need to be hyper-vigilant and monitor everything. It’s like a closed loop: they treat him as the authority figure, and he responds by acting like he’s in charge of the medical outcome, and I’m just left there, trying to exist in the middle of it all. 

I’ve never had anyone ask, "How can we support *Antionette* right now?" It’s always "We’re going to do this for the baby's safety." I feel like a container that needs to be properly managed until the task is complete. It doesn't make me feel included; it makes me feel like I’m the problem that’s being solved by a team of experts—and my husband is their consultant.
Tell me about a moment during your birth when a decision needed to be made quickly or under pressure. Who was involved in that decision, and how did you experience your role in it?

During my second birth, there was a point where the fetal heart rate decelerated—just for a minute, but it felt like a lifetime. The room went from a quiet, low-lit space to being filled with three extra nurses and a doctor, all moving in perfect, clinical sync. I was in the middle of a contraction, and suddenly I was being told to shift positions, have oxygen placed on me, and then questioned about my hydration levels.

The decision to increase the monitoring and start a pitocin drip was essentially made *at* me, not with me. My husband, bless him, was standing at the edge of the circle, looking like he was about to pass out, while the doctor barked, "We need to do this to ensure stability." 

In that moment, I felt zero agency. I wasn’t a person; I was a machine that had momentarily malfunctioned and needed recalibration. I remember wanting to scream, "Just give me a second!" but I was too terrified of the consequence of delaying. I felt like if I said no, I was responsible for whatever "distress" followed. So, I just nodded. I let them do what they needed. It worked, the baby was fine, but that lingering feeling of being a passive recipient of "care"—where I was completely bypassed in the decision-making process—still haunts me. I was the one doing the labor, but I wasn't the one in charge.

Tell me about how the physical environment of your birth — the room, the equipment, the sounds and smells — felt in relation to the people around you. How did the space and the relationships interact?

The room felt like an operating theater that was trying too hard to look like a bedroom. There were these soft, muted colors on the wall, but they were drowned out by the sterile, clinical hardware—the fetal monitor that chirped every few seconds, the IV pole, the tray of stainless steel instruments. It felt like a trap. Every time I looked at those monitors, I wasn't just seeing data; I was seeing a scorecard.

The interaction between the space and the people was incredibly jarring. The nurses would move through the room with such clinical efficiency that they didn't seem to notice the "softer" elements of the space at all. They’d bump into my yoga ball or step over my bag without a second thought. For them, it was just a workspace. But for me? It was the space where my body was being opened up. 

When Mark stood by the monitor, the room felt smaller, more claustrophobic. It felt like a triangle of surveillance: the monitor, Mark watching the monitor, and the doctor overseeing us both. I felt like I had to inhabit that tiny, high-pressure center. The "homey" parts of the room—the dim lights, the chair in the corner—felt like a cruel mockery when I was attached to a heart rate monitor and being told to adjust my breathing to match the medical protocol. I wanted to just be a woman giving birth, not a patient being processed in a room that was pretending to be something it wasn't.
Who else was involved in shaping that environment — did anyone advocate for changes to make the space feel safer or more personal?

No one. That’s the part that hurts to even admit. I think I assumed the responsibility of being the "perfect patient" meant that I shouldn't be the one asking for changes. If I asked to dim the lights further, or to move the monitor so Mark wasn't staring at it like a hawk, would they have thought I wasn't taking the "medium risk" monitoring seriously enough?

I felt like an intruder in their space, not the other way around. It’s their room, their equipment, their protocols—I was just the one who had to pass through it. I remember looking at the harsh fluorescent lights above the bed and wanting to ask if we could turn them off, but I stayed quiet. I didn't want to be the "difficult" one, the one who cared more about ambiance than "safety." 

In retrospect, that’s so sad. Why did I equate "safe" with "clinical and uncomfortable"? I think I was so afraid of a bad outcome that I was willing to sacrifice every ounce of my own comfort. I let the environment define the terms of the birth instead of defining it myself. I stayed in that rigid, medicalized box because I was too afraid to ask for a window or a softer chair, fearing that any deviation from the standard routine would somehow invite disaster.

Tell me about the people who knew about your birth preferences or plan beforehand — your midwife, your partner, your family — and how well those preferences were known and respected in the room on the day.

I had this beautiful, color-coded birth plan. I’d researched everything—delayed cord clamping, immediate skin-to-skin, the works. I handed it to my midwife at 36 weeks, and she smiled and said, "We’ll do our best, Antionette." But when the intensity of labor actually hit, that plan didn't feel like a roadmap; it felt like a fragile piece of paper that was easily ignored.

My husband knew the plan, but once the monitoring started and things got "high stakes," he forgot it entirely. He was so focused on the monitors that when I’d ask for something from the plan—like dimming the lights or holding off on a certain intervention—he’d dismiss it as a "distraction." He thought he was helping by prioritizing the medical "safety" over my wishes. And the midwife? She was wonderful, but when the shift change happened, the new OB came in with her own checklist. She didn't even look at my plan. She walked in, saw the monitors, and started the clinical routine. 

It wasn't a malicious disregard. It was more like they were all operating on a different frequency than me. My preferences felt like "nice-to-haves" that were easily discarded the second the reality of a "medium risk" birth set in. I felt like I was shouting into a void, trying to advocate for my own body while they were all focused on the "proper" medical way to bring the baby out.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

When I was first pregnant with my oldest, I had a family doctor—a woman who had known me since I was a teenager. I remember walking into her office, terrified and barely showing, and she didn't just pull out a chart. She poured me a cup of tea and asked me how *I* was feeling, not how my blood pressure was. That was the last time I felt truly seen in a medical setting.

I trusted her because she wasn't looking at me like a data set. She didn't have that "medium risk" label or any label at all; she just had a human connection with me. I felt like I could tell her anything—even the irrational, 3:00 AM fears—and she wouldn't judge me. She wasn't just my doctor; she was a partner.

Now, though? That trust feels like a luxury I can no longer afford. Because I’m "medium risk," I’ve been passed into the hands of specialists who see me as a project to be managed. I miss that feeling of being a whole person to my provider, rather than a collection of vitals and a, "You’re doing great, Antionette," delivered with a hand on the doorknob. I think I’ve lost that sense of connection because I’ve been trained to believe that clinical distance is the price I have to pay for a "safe" outcome. It’s a steep price, isn't it?

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

Oh, definitely. Before I was even pregnant with my first, I went to see my doctor about preconception planning. I had this vision of a conversation where we’d talk about lifestyle, maybe some holistic ways to prepare my body, just a general check-in. Instead, it was this rapid-fire list of tests: blood panels, genetic screenings, iron levels—all these things that needed to be "optimized" before I could even try.

I remember saying, "I’m not sure I’m ready for all that testing right now," and the look I got was... well, it was the look of a doctor who thought I was being irresponsible. She didn't ask me *why* I felt that way; she just laid out the "necessary" protocols. In that office, the decision was already made for me: if I wanted to be "healthy" and have a "safe" pregnancy, I had to follow the checklist. There was no room to say, "Let’s wait," or "I want to take a different approach."

It set the tone, honestly. It made me feel like my own intuition was secondary to the clinical standard. I walked out of there feeling like I was already behind schedule, already failing, and already losing control over my own reproductive life. It’s been that way ever since—a subtle, constant nudge toward whatever the system deems the "right" path, leaving me to wonder if I ever really had a say at all.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

Before I was pregnant with my first, I felt like my body was something I owned. It was a tool, something that did what I asked of it—I ran, I worked long hours, I felt strong. I had this romantic, slightly naive idea that pregnancy would be this natural extension of that strength. I didn't think about "risk" or "complications" or the clinical machine; I just thought about a growing bump and the miracle of it all.

I didn't talk about it openly with many people. I think I was scared to admit that I was actually quite intimidated by the *loss* of control. My friends who were already mothers would just laugh and say, "You just wait, Antionette, you'll see." It felt like a secret club I was being inducted into where the price of admission was your own body's autonomy.

Now, looking back, I realize how much I was holding back. I wanted to talk about the fear of the medical gaze, the fear that once I became a "patient," I wouldn't be Antionette anymore. But there’s no place to have that conversation without sounding like you’re ungrateful. So, I kept it all internal. I thought I could outsmart the system by staying fit and healthy, but none of that prepares you for the moment you’re labeled "medium risk" and suddenly, you’re just a file on a desk.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

I’m a researcher by nature, so I started with the big guns: peer-reviewed journals, PubMed, clinical guidelines from the ACOG. If a source didn't have a clear methodology or if it was just some "mom-fluencer" trying to sell a supplement, I’d dismiss it immediately. I wanted the cold, hard data because I thought, *if I have the same data the doctors have, they can’t dismiss me.*

But even then, it felt like I was collecting pieces of a puzzle that didn't quite fit together. I trusted sources that focused on physiology—how cells divide, how blood volume changes—because that felt objective. But I avoided the "you’ll just know, listen to your heart" types of advice. I thought that was dangerous. I felt like if I relied on my intuition and was wrong, it would be my fault, but if I relied on data and things went wrong, well, at least I’d done everything "by the book."

It’s ironic now, looking back. I spent so much time curating these "trustworthy" sources, but they never told me how to cope with the anxiety of *being* a patient. They gave me facts, but they didn't give me context for my own life. I built this massive library of medical knowledge, but when I’m sitting in the exam room, all that data just makes me feel more isolated. I was trying to out-calculate the risk, but the risk—or the feeling of it—is everywhere.
Did you ever come across advice or information that felt hard to access — perhaps because of cost, language, or just not knowing where to start?

The information that’s truly hard to access isn't the clinical data—that's easy enough to find if you know how to read a journal. The *real* information, the kind I’ve been starving for, is the "insider" stuff. Like, how to talk to a doctor when they're rushing you, or how to advocate for a change in procedure without sounding like a "difficult" patient. That stuff isn't on any hospital website. 

It’s about the culture of care. I’ve had to piece together bits of advice from anonymous forums, reading between the lines of what other women say when they talk about their birth experiences. It’s a hidden language of tips and warnings that you only get if you know where to dig. 

I’ve also felt a huge barrier when it comes to mental health. I’ve looked for support that isn't just "take this pill" or "you’re just having hormonal mood swings." It’s so expensive to find a therapist who specializes in birth trauma or perinatal anxiety, and most of them don't take insurance. It feels like this invisible wall—if you’re not in a certain tax bracket, you’re just expected to white-knuckle your way through the anxiety because "that’s just what being a mom is." It’s infuriating that I have to pay to be treated like a whole person.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

Even before I was officially "medium risk," I was already hyper-aware of my age. Being thirty-one feels young in some ways, but in the sterile, checkbox-driven world of the clinic, you start to feel the clock ticking the second you mention you want a baby. I felt this subtle pressure that my body was a project that had to be "optimized"—that my weight, my stress levels, my lifestyle choices were all data points that could be used against me if something didn't go "perfectly."

It’s humiliating, honestly. I remember standing in front of my doctor, and instead of asking if I was excited or how I felt about becoming a mother, she started discussing the statistical "risks" of my age group. It made me feel like my own biology was a ticking bomb. I felt like I was already apologising for my existence before the baby was even conceived. It turns your body into something you’re constantly defending, rather than something you’re living in. I became so self-conscious that I started viewing every ache, every cycle, every moment of fatigue as a potential "problem." It made me feel like my body wasn't a partner, but a liability I had to manage so the medical establishment wouldn't catch me doing it "wrong." That’s a heavy, lonely way to start a journey that’s supposed to be about creation.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

Mark was involved, but honestly, his involvement felt more like "project management." Before we started, he was all about the logistics—the timeline, the finances, the "readiness" check. He was the one who encouraged me to get the preconception screenings, the one who looked at my charts and said, "This seems like the most logical path."

I think he thought he was being supportive by making the process "efficient," but it just made me feel like we were planning a business merger. Whenever I tried to bring up the emotional side—the fear that my body wouldn't handle it, or the anxiety about handing over my autonomy to the medical system—he’d pivot back to the "facts." He’d say things like, "We’re doing the right thing, we’re following the doctor’s advice, what else is there to worry about?"

It left me feeling like the "emotional" part of the journey was just an inefficiency I needed to fix. I felt so alone in those conversations, even though he was right there. He didn't want to hear about the fear; he wanted to hear that we had a plan. So, I learned to keep the fear to myself and only talk about the schedule. It felt like I was already starting to lose the ability to be honest about my own experience, all for the sake of keeping the "project" on track.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

Mark came to the initial consultations, and honestly, it changed everything—just not in the way I wanted. When it was just me and the doctor, the conversation felt like a negotiation. I’d try to probe about protocols or ask for alternatives to standard testing. But the moment Mark walked in, the doctor’s entire demeanor shifted. She’d turn her chair toward him, explaining the "risks" and "necessities" as if he were the one who needed to sign off on the work.

Mark would nod, fully satisfied with the technical explanation, and say, "Okay, that sounds like the right call, doctor." And just like that, the conversation was over. I’d be left there, feeling completely sidelined, wanting to ask another question but feeling like the "difficult" one for reopening a topic that the "man of the house" had already agreed to. 

It made me feel like I was a passenger, not the one carrying the pregnancy. I’d sit there biting my tongue, watching them map out the future of my own body like they were discussing a home renovation project. It made me feel so small. I wanted him there as a witness to my humanity, not as a second set of ears for the clinical team to convince. It’s hard to feel like an equal partner when you’re being treated like an observer in your own medical life.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

There was this one time, years ago, before I was pregnant, when I had a minor injury and went to a clinic. The doctor—a man, actually—asked me a question about how I wanted to approach the healing process, giving me two options: a conservative, time-heavy approach or a quicker, more invasive one. He listened while I weighed the pros and cons, and when I chose the slower path, he didn't blink. He just said, "It’s your body, Antionette. I’m just here to make sure you have the info to decide." I remember leaving that room feeling like a grown adult. It was such a small interaction, but I felt so empowered.

Contrast that with my first preconception visit. I brought up my concerns about being over-monitored, and the doctor’s eyes just glazed over. She started citing percentages and "best practice" protocols as if I hadn't spoken at all. I tried to push back, but she cut me off with, "We have to follow the standard of care to ensure the best outcome." 

In that moment, my voice didn't matter. It felt like I was a malfunctioning appliance she needed to troubleshoot. It was the start of realizing that the maternity system isn't really built for *my* choices; it’s built for *their* protocols. It’s like being in a conversation where the other person has already decided what you’re going to say. It’s incredibly lonely to realize that your own life is being managed by a checklist that you didn't even help write.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

When I started this, I thought I could "out-research" the system. I thought if I knew more than the average patient, I would naturally be treated as an equal partner. I wanted to be a collaborator. But as the "medium risk" label took hold, I realized that knowledge wasn't a seat at the table—it was just a way to make myself more anxious.

My wishes haven't changed, but my *expectations* have shifted. I’ve gone from wanting a collaborative partner to just wanting someone who doesn't treat me like an object. I’ve realized that being "in charge" of my body in this environment feels like an illusion. The system is designed to funnel me toward their standard protocols, and every time I try to assert my own wishes, I’m met with a wall of "policy" or "best practice."

It’s heartbreaking, honestly. I’ve stopped trying to be the informed collaborator and I’m just trying to survive the next few weeks without losing my identity entirely. I still want my wishes respected, but I’ve learned that the cost of that is often being labeled "difficult" or "anxious." So, I keep quiet, I perform the role of the compliant patient, and I save my true voice for those 3:00 AM online threads where other women are doing the same thing. I’m exhausted by the effort of trying to be "in charge" when the system is clearly holding the wheel.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

I think the moments when I felt respected were surprisingly rare. There was one time at a routine check-up, my usual midwife was out, and I had someone I hadn't met before. I was spiraling about something—I think it was about fetal movement patterns—and I started to pull out my notebook with all my data points. I braced myself for the usual patronizing "you're worrying too much" response. Instead, she just sat down, put her hands in her lap, and said, "You know your baby better than anyone, Antionette. What does your gut tell you?"

I almost started crying. No one had asked me about my gut in months; they’d only asked about my blood pressure and my glucose logs. In that split second, I felt like a human being again, not a "medium risk" statistic. She didn't dismiss the data I had, but she acknowledged that *I* was the expert on my own body. It was such a small, human gesture, and yet it felt like a lifeline.

But then, the flip side: nearly every other interaction feels like a lecture. When I express a preference—like wanting to hold off on a certain elective screening or asking for a different approach to my nutrition—the conversation shuts down. It’s like, "Antionette, we’ve been doing this for years, and our protocol says X, Y, and Z." It’s a dismissal. It’s as if my history, my body, and my intuition are just noise in the system that they need to filter out so they can get to the "correct" clinical outcome. That’s when I know my voice doesn't count—when the protocol becomes more important than the person it’s supposed to be protecting.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

My husband, Mark, has been involved, but it feels like he’s playing a role written by the hospital, not one we wrote together. He’s the one who shows up at the appointments, clipboard in hand, ready to cross off the items on the hospital’s list. In the beginning, I thought that was "support." Now, I see it as him being the system's loudest advocate.

He’s so terrified of something going wrong that he’s become the gatekeeper of the "correct" medical path. When I’ve tried to raise questions about, say, the necessity of a specific ultrasound or why they're pushing for a certain protocol, he just looks at me with that frantic, protective edge and says, "Why are you questioning the doctor? They’re the experts, Antionette." It’s like he’s terrified that if I push back, I’m inviting disaster.

I feel incredibly lonely because of it. I’m the one carrying this baby, the one feeling every shift and every change in my body, but I’m constantly having to "manage" him so he doesn't panic. His involvement isn't about empowering me; it’s about his own need for control through the system. I want him as a partner, not as a representative for the clinical team. It makes me feel like I’m outnumbered in my own pregnancy, and that’s a bitter, isolating pill to swallow when you're already feeling so vulnerable.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

Oh, absolutely. It’s like a performance art piece we haven’t rehearsed, but we both know the script. When I’m alone with the doctor, I’m much more likely to ask the "uncomfortable" questions—like the ones about the actual necessity of certain tests or why they’re labeling me "medium risk" so strictly. I feel like I have the space to advocate for my own body, even if they don't love it.

But the second Mark walks in, the energy shifts. The doctor stops explaining *why* something is happening and starts giving a summary—a "TL;DR" for the husband. And Mark, in his well-meaning but frantic way, reinforces that. He’ll say, "Okay, sounds like a plan, thanks for explaining that," and then he’ll give me this look that says, *See? It’s all settled.* 

I end up silencing myself. I don't want to challenge the doctor in front of him because I know he’ll get that panicked look, and then I’ll have to spend the car ride home de-escalating *him* instead of focusing on what I actually need. His presence feels like a muzzle. I go from being a patient who wants to know the "why" to a quiet bystander who just nods. It’s supposed to be "support," but it feels like I’ve lost my best advocate. I’m just trying to make it to the end without breaking, and having a second person there just makes the pressure to conform feel ten times heavier.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

It’s been this strange, painful tug-of-war. On one hand, I’m hyper-aware of every physiological shift—the way the baby moves, the rhythm of my own pulse, the way my ribs feel like they’re expanding to make space. I feel a deep, biological connection to this little life, a kind of primal protectiveness that I didn’t know I was capable of. That part is beautiful.

But on the other hand, my body has become a public utility. I feel like it belongs to the clinicians, to the insurance companies, to the data charts. I’m constantly "managing" it—adjusting my diet, tracking my movements, worrying about my glucose—until I feel like I’m just an observer living in a shell that’s being managed by someone else. I’ve lost that sense of ownership I used to have. 

I haven’t felt safe talking about this with anyone in my real life. If I told Mark, he’d just worry. If I told my friends, they’d tell me I’m overthinking the "miracle of life." So, I’m left with these internal monologues, sitting in the kitchen, feeling this profound sense of alienation. I crave a space where I can admit that I’m struggling to inhabit my own skin right now—without it being "fixed" or "labeled." Just to be heard. I think that's the only thing that would make me feel like *me* again.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

There was a moment during a growth scan that sticks with me. The ultrasound tech—a woman who was otherwise quite professional—tossed out a comment, just casually, while looking at the screen: "You’re really carrying quite small for a third baby, aren't you? We’ll have to watch that weight closely." 

It was meant to be clinical, probably a "just doing my job" observation, but it felt like a direct indictment of my body’s capabilities. I’d been working so hard to hit my protein targets, obsessing over every snack to ensure my glucose stayed stable, and to have her imply my body was failing to nourish the baby properly… it was like a punch to the stomach. 

I didn't say anything, of course. I just felt this wave of shame. My husband, who was sitting right there, just nodded and said, "Yeah, we’ve been tracking that, so let us know if we need to do more." He thought he was being helpful, but he was just confirming the idea that my body was a project that wasn't performing to spec. It’s like everyone in that room was looking at my bump as a data point that needed correction. I spent the entire drive home feeling like a failure, wondering if my body was, in fact, "small" or "inadequate." Even now, I can’t stop checking the scale. Those casual, judgmental remarks are the ones that burrow deep.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the most important thing I can say is that the "medium risk" label is a quiet thief. It steals the joy and replaces it with a constant, humming anxiety that you aren't allowed to express because you're supposed to be "lucky" to be monitored so closely. People hear "medium risk" and they think "good, at least they’re being thorough." They don’t see the exhaustion of being treated like a broken machine that needs constant calibration.

I really wish that the system—and the people around me—understood that when a mother asks questions, it’s not always because she’s anxious or "difficult." Sometimes it’s because she’s trying to reclaim her agency in a space that’s doing everything to strip it away. I don't want to be "managed." I want to be *seen*. I want to be able to talk about my fears without them being dismissed as "hormonal" or "overthinking." 

There’s this profound silence that surrounds the pregnant woman’s internal experience. Everyone is so obsessed with the baby—the heartbeat, the growth, the kicks—that the woman carrying them becomes invisible. If there’s one thing that could make maternity care better, it would be someone just asking, "How are *you* doing with all of this?" And then actually waiting for the answer. That would have changed everything for me.
